rationale: The page provides detailed historical context on major Constitutional Amendments (38th, 39th, 42nd, 44th) and a significant Supreme Court judgment regarding the assent to state bills under Articles 200/201. These are high-yield topics for polity exams.
cleanedText:
Major Constitutional Amendments and Reforms During and After Emergency
Timeline of Amendments:
- 1975: 38th Amendment - Barred judicial review of Emergency declaration.
- 1975: 39th Amendment - Parliament empowered to decide election disputes.
- 1976: 42nd Amendment - Sweeping changes including primacy to Directive Principles, curtailment of judicial powers, extended Lok Sabha term.
- 1977: Shah Commission - Probe into Emergency abuses and excesses.
- 1978: 44th Amendment - Replaced "internal disturbance" with "armed rebellion," restored judicial review.
Other Changes brought through 44th Amendment Act (1978):
- For Emergency: Union Cabinet to communicate to President in writing.
- Mandatory Parliamentary approval within a month by special majority for Emergency Proclamations.
- Restricted scope of Article 359 i.e. Article 20 and Article 21 remain enforceable during emergency.
- Article 358 automatically suspended rights under Article 19 (only for Emergency imposed due to war or external aggression).
- Omitted right to property as FR and made it Constitutional right under Article 300A.
- Removal of Article 257A: Allowed Government of India to deploy any armed force of Union or any other force to deal with any grave situation of law and order in any State.
- Term of Lok Sabha: Back to 5 years from 6 years by amending Articles 83 and 172.
- Restored judicial review: To review election disputes concerning President and Vice-President.
1.2. ASSENT TO STATE BILLS
Why in the News?
Supreme Court in 16th Presidential Reference (under Article 143) held that Court cannot impose any timelines for decisions of President and Governor on granting assent to Bills under Articles 200/201 of Constitution.
Key Highlights of Judgment on 16th Presidential Reference
- ‘Deemed Assent’ Unconstitutional: Courts cannot assume assent to Bill merely because court-ordered time lapsed, as it infringes constitutional powers of President/Governor, violates separation of powers and federal constitutional scheme.
- Governors/President Cannot Sit on Bills Indefinitely: As it violates federalism, legislature’s will and constitutional morality.
- Limited Judicial Review: Courts can intervene if Governor/President acts with malafide intent and review is confined to procedure, not merits.
- Supreme Advice not required: President need not seek Supreme Court’s advice whenever Governor reserves a Bill for his/her assent as subjective satisfaction of President is sufficient.
